concepts.title: Properties of group 16
concepts.conceptBody:
Group 16 (VIA-The Chalcogens)

  • The first four elements are called chalcogens meaning ore forming (oxides, sulphides, selenides, tellurides).
  • Similar to the groups discussed earlier, the metallic character in Group 16 increases with increasing atomic number. Oxygen and sulphur are entirely non-metallic in their chemical behaviour. Selenium and tellurium though essentially non-metallic, assume increasing metallic character and are termed as metalloids. Polonium is most metallic in the group.

Density, melting and boiling points covalent and ionic radii, show a regular increase from oxygen to polonium. The large difference in melting and boiling points between oxygen and sulphur can be explained on the basis of their structure. Oxygen exists mostly as a diatomic molecule held together by weak van der Waals forces while others exist as polyatomic molecules, e.g., ${{\text{S}}_{8}},\text{S}{{\text{e}}_{8}}$, etc., where the atoms are bonded by covalent bonds associated with high dissociation energy. Their existence as diatomic and polyatomic molecules can be explained as follows. The bond energy of the oxygen-oxygen double bond. $\text{O}=\text{O}$, is $498~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$. This makes the $\text{O}=\text{O}$ bond more than three times as strong as the $\text{O}-\text{O}$ bond (bond energy for $\text{O}-\text{O}$ is $142~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$ ). By comparison the $\text{S}=\text{S}$ bond is less than twice as strong as the $\text{S}-\text{S}$ single bond (bond energy for $\text{S}=\text{S},431~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}};\text{S}-\text{S},265~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$ ). This results in catenated $-\text{O}-\text{O}-\text{O}-$ chains beginning unstable relative to $\text{O}=\text{O}$, but catenated $-\text{S}-\text{S}-\text{S}-$ chains being stable relative to the molecule $\text{S}=\text{S}$.

The elements of Group 16 are characterised by high ionisation energies. Their electronegativities decrease with increasing atomic number. Thus, in view of the fall in electronegativity, metallic character within the group increases with increasing atomic size.

Oxygen, the second most electronegative element, fluorine being the first, has a strong tendency to accept two electrons and give ${{\text{O}}^{2-}}$ ion. Thus, almost all metal oxides are ionic and contain ${{\text{O}}^{2-}}$ ions. Usually oxygen exhibits an oxidation state -2 in its other compounds also. It exhibits positive oxidation states only in a few compounds formed with fluorine, i.e., $\text{O}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{O}}_{2}}~{{\text{F}}_{2}}$. The tendency for the formation of divalent anions decreases from sulphur downwards because of the increasing size and decreasing electronegativity of the elements. Sulphur, selenium an tellurium show a tendency for covalent with formal oxidation states $+2,+4$ and +6 in compounds in which they are combined with more electronegative elements such as oxygen and halogens. In the higher oxidation states of +4 and +6 of these elements electrons are unpaired and promoted to vacant d orbitals.

  • Selenium has unique property of photoconductivity and is used in photocopying (xerox) machines and also a decoloriser of glass. Te and Po are highly toxic. Po is higher due to its intense radioactivity. Se and $\text{Te}$ are found as selemides and tellurides in sulphide ores $\left( \text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{Se},\text{C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{Te} \right)$. The colour of the red glass in the traffic signals is due to CdSe.
  • With valence electronic configuration $\text{n}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\text{n}{{\text{p}}^{4}}$ the group 16 (VI A) elements are just two electrons short of an octet configuration, and -2 oxidation state is therefore, a common one. The stability of 2 state decreases with increasing metallic character as indicated by $\text{E}$ (redox) values. This oxygen is powerful oxidising agent and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ Se and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Te}$ are reducing agent ( $\text{E}$ values of $\text{Se}$ and $\text{Te}$ are negative). Because $\text{S}$, Se, and $\text{Te}$ are much less electronegative than oxygen, they are commonly found in positive oxidation states as +4 in $\text{S}{{\text{F}}_{4}},\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$, and +6 in $\text{S}{{\text{F}}_{6}},\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}},{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$
  • ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Se}$ and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Te}$ are unpleasant gaseous hydrides like ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$ but less stable than ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$, order being ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}>{{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}>{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Se}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Te}>{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Po}$.
  • Oxoacids of $\text{S}$, Se and Te exist and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ and ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Se}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ and considered as strong acids.
  • The tendency for catenation decreases markedly as we go down the group. This property is prominently displayed by sulphur $\left( {{S}_{8}} \right)$. The $S-S$ bond is important in biological system and is found in compounds such as cysteine, some proteins and enzymes. Stable polyoxides and polysulphides are known. Examples are:

$\text{H}-\text{O}-\text{O}-\text{H},\text{H}-\text{S}-\text{S}-\text{H},$$\text{H}-\text{S}-\text{S}-\text{S}-\text{H},$ $H-S-S-S-S-H.\text{ }$

  • The metallic character increases as we descend the group.$\text{S}$is non-metal and insulator. Se and Te are metalloids and are semiconductors. Po is a metal.
  • Unlike oxygen, the maximum coordination number of sulphur and other elements can exceed four and valency is not limited to two because of available d-orbitals for bonding. Stability of +6 state decreases and that of +4 state increases going down the group due to inert-pair effect. It is:

$\text{S}{{\text{e}}^{4+}}<\text{T}{{\text{e}}^{4+}}<\text{P}{{\text{o}}^{4+}}$

$\text{S}{{\text{e}}^{6+}}>\text{T}{{\text{e}}^{6+}}>\text{P}{{\text{o}}^{6+}}$

  • The tendency to form multiple bonds to$\text{C},\text{N}$ and $\text{O}$ decreases as we descend the group from Se to Te. Thus $\text{S}=\text{C}=\text{S}$ is moderately stable; $\text{Se}=\text{C}=\text{Se}$ decomposes readily whereas $\text{Te}=\text{C}=\text{Te}$ does not exist.
  • Thermal stability of the hyrdies decreases as we go down the group:

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}>{{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}>{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Se}>{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Te}>{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Po}$

  • They form halides of different oxidation number + 1, + 2, + 4 and + 6 . 

The stability of the halides decreases in the order $\text{F}>\text{Cl}>\text{Br}>\text{I}.+6$ oxidation is observed in fluroide, while iodide of $\text{Te}\left( \text{Te}{{\text{I}}_{4}} \right)$ is formed.

$\text{S}{{\text{F}}_{6}}$ is thermodynamically inert, non-toxic gas. Inertness of $\text{S}{{\text{F}}_{6}}$ is due to the presence of sterically protected sulplhur atom. $\text{S}{{\text{F}}_{6}}$ is used as a gaseous insulator in high voltage generators. $\text{S}{{\text{F}}_{4}}$ and $\text{Se}{{\text{F}}_{4}}$ are good fluorinating agents and $-\text{COOH}$ group can be converted into $\text{C}{{\text{F}}_{3}}$ and $>\text{C}=\text{O}$ and $-\text{P}=\text{O}$ into $\text{C}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ and $-\text{P}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ groups.

  • Oxides of sulphur are more stable than the corresponding oxides of other elements.

concepts.title: Ozone
concepts.conceptBody:
Ozone $\left( {{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}} \right)$

  • In the laboratory, ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$ can be made by passing oxygen through a strong electric field. An equilibrium is set up:

$3{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{s})\rightleftharpoons 2{{\text{O}}_{3}}(~\text{g})$

  • Some$30~\text{km}$ above the earth's surface oxygen molecules can be split apart by UV light from the sun. Some of the atoms join with other oxygen molecules to make ozone

${{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+\text{O}(\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(~\text{g})$

  • ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$is an unstable, dark blue diamagnetic gas, b.p. -112 C. The colour is due to intense absorption of red light.

$\text{NO}+{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{ }$

${{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{hv}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{O}$

$\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{NO}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$2{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{h}v\xrightarrow{{}}3{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Net reaction: $2{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{h}\nu \xrightarrow{{}}3{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

- based on reactive chlorine species from CFC

$\text{Cl}+{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ClO}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

${{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{hv}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{O}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\text{ClO}+\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Cl}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Net reaction: $2{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{hv}\xrightarrow{{}}3{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

  • ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$ is thermodynamically unstable, and decomposes to ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$:

$2{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}3{{\text{O}}_{2}}\quad \Delta \text{G}=-163~\text{kJ}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$

  • ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$ is an extremely powerful oxidising agent:

${{\text{O}}_{3}}+2{{\text{H}}^{+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

${{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{O}}_{2}}+2\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

$3\text{PbS}+4{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}3\text{PbS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\text{ }$

$6\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}3~{{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}$

$\text{S}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{O}}_{3}}\text{ }\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

$2\text{KOH}+5{{\text{O}}_{3}}\text{ }\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{K}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+5{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

${{\text{O}}_{3}}+2\text{KI}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}+2\text{KOH}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$3\text{SnC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+6\text{HCl}+{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}3\text{SnC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$2{{\left[ \text{Fe}{{(\text{CN})}_{6}} \right]}^{4-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}2{{\left[ \text{Fe}{{(\text{CN})}_{6}} \right]}^{3-}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

Potassium ozonide $\text{K}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ is an orange coloured solid and contains the paramagnetic $\text{O}_{3}^{-}$ion.

  • The amount of ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$ in a gas mixture can be determined by passing the gas into a KI solution (at a constant $\text{pH}9.2$ using borate buffer). The iodine that is liberated is titrated with sodium thiosulphate solution.

${{\text{O}}_{3}}+2{{\text{I}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}+2\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

${{\text{I}}_{2}}+2~{{\text{S}}_{2}}\text{O}_{3}^{2-}\xrightarrow{{}}2{{\text{I}}^{-}}+{{\text{S}}_{4}}\text{O}_{6}^{2-}$

  • ${{O}_{3}}$ also adds to unsaturated organic compounds at room temperature forming ozonides which can be cleaved to aldehydes and ketones in solution:

  • ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$ is used as a disinfectant; it is used to purify drinking water since, it can destroy bacteria and viruses. It is better than $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ since, it $\left( {{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)$ avoids the unpleasant smell and taste of $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ and any excess ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$ soon decomposes to ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$.

$\text{CH}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{{H}_{2}}O/Zn}2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CHO}$

The molecules${{\text{O}}_{3}}$ is bent with an OOO angle 116.8 and equal $\text{O}-\text{O}$ distance of $128\text{pm}$ and may be described 90 canonical forms.
